coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical and surface investigations of N, S codoped carbon dots as effective corrosion inhibitor for mild steel in acidic solution

coredata.dc:description: Mild steel is a versatile, inexpensive, highly flexible, and easily weldable material widely used in construction, pipelines, transportation, shipping, and other industries. However, it is also extremely susceptible to corrosion processes under the influence of weather phenomena, posing a significant threat to society and human health. In this work, we investigated the corrosion inhibition capacity of a new type of N, S codoped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) on Q235 carbon steel in the acidic medium. The goal product was synthesized by hydrothermal method using o-phenylenediamine and amidinothiourea as precursors. Combined with electrochemistry, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, scanning electrochemical microscopy (SECM), and theoretical calculations, the inhibition properties of N, S-CDs on Q235 carbon steel in HCl solution were evaluated. The potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) curves and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) results indicated that the corrosion inhibition efficiency reached 90.3 % and 92.4 % at 100 mg/L concentration, respectively. Indeed, the presence of N, S-CDs reduced the electric double-layer capacitance, increased the thickness of the adsorption film on the carbon steel surface, and reduced the corrosion current density, resulting in a delay of corrosion. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis and surface characterization confirmed the successful doping of N, S and the formation of adsorption films, the Langmuir adsorption isotherm also confirmed the superior inhibition of N, S-CDs on the surface of Q235 carbon steel through physical and chemical adsorption mechanisms. At the same time, quantum chemical calculations and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations revealed its inhibition mechanism at the molecular/atomic level.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mild steel is a versatile, inexpensive, highly flexible, and easily weldable material widely used in construction, pipelines, transportation, shipping, and other industries. However, it is also extremely susceptible to corrosion processes under the influence of weather phenomena, posing a significant threat to society and human health. In this work, we investigated the corrosion inhibition capacity of a new type of N, S codoped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) on Q235 carbon steel in the acidic medium. The goal product was synthesized by hydrothermal method using o-phenylenediamine and amidinothiourea as precursors. Combined with electrochemistry, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, scanning electrochemical microscopy (SECM), and theoretical calculations, the inhibition properties of N, S-CDs on Q235 carbon steel in HCl solution were evaluated. The potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) curves and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) results indicated that the corrosion inhibition efficiency reached 90.3 % and 92.4 % at 100 mg/L concentration, respectively. Indeed, the presence of N, S-CDs reduced the electric double-layer capacitance, increased the thickness of the adsorption film on the carbon steel surface, and reduced the corrosion current density, resulting in a delay of corrosion. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis and surface characterization confirmed the successful doping of N, S and the formation of adsorption films, the Langmuir adsorption isotherm also confirmed the superior inhibition of N, S-CDs on the surface of Q235 carbon steel through physical and chemical adsorption mechanisms. At the same time, quantum chemical calculations and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations revealed its inhibition mechanism at the molecular/atomic level.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The use of metals has found wide diffusion across different sectors, from industrial to mechanical levels, production technology, electronics, construction, and materials for everyday use. However, metals are susceptible to destructive processes caused by corrosion. Q235 carbon steel is extensively used in the petrochemical industry, machinery manufacturing, and other fields. It is prone to corrosion in acidic environments, leading to damage and deterioration, resulting in economic losses and environmental pollution [1,2]. The mitigation of corrosion during metal pickling is of great importance. The most widely used method is the addition of inhibitors, which prevent corrosion by adsorbing onto the metal surface. Small doses of these molecules assure efficient protection at low cost [3,4]. So far, among the various corrosion inhibitors available, organic compounds, like Schiff bases, ionic liquids, and imidazole molecules, are often used for corrosion protection [5–7]. However, their high toxicity and refractory degradation increase environmental pollution. To overcome these limits, new green, efficient, and soluble in-water solution corrosion inhibitors have been proposed. According to research, carbon dots (CDs) are a special kind of graphene-liked nanomaterials, that have shown excellent water solubility, low toxicity, biocompatibility, and fluorescence performance [8,9]. CDs contain double bonds and electronegative atoms, such as N, O, and S atoms, which can enhance adsorption on the surface and inhibit metal corrosion more effectively [10]. Research has shown that the presence of functional groups on the molecular structure of inhibitors enhances the interaction between heteroatoms and the atoms of the metal substrate. Therefore, in the synthesis of CDs, the molecular structure is modified to introduce the desired functional group, to improve the chemical adsorption characteristics of the material [11]. The doping procedure of nitrogen atoms represents an excellent choice. Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) maintain the advantage of CDs, together with the benefits of the N heteroatoms, which can effectively decrease the corrosion velocity of metal in an acidic environment [12]. So far, Cui et al. discovered for the first time that nitrogen-doped carbon dots synthesized with aminosalicylic acid as the precursor system, have excellent corrosion inhibition activity on carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution [13]. Denis et al. have shown that double doping of N and S elements improves the reactivity of graphene favoring the adsorption with metals (K, Na, and Li) [14,15]. Chen and colleagues found that at a concentration of 50 ppm, N, S-CDs had an inhibition efficiency of about 93 % for carbon steel in a 3.5 wt% sodium chloride solution [16]. Nevertheless, as novel corrosion inhibitors in acidic conditions, research on carbon dots needs additional development and investigation.

Due to the large number of active groups on the surface of N, S-CDs, such as hydroxyl, amino, and sulfhydryl groups, these groups can have a strong interaction with the metal surface to form a stable adsorption film, thereby effectively inhibiting the contact between the corrosive medium and the metal surface, and slowing down the corrosion process of the metal. Several investigations have demonstrated that N, S-CDs have outstanding corrosion inhibition ability against a range of metals (such as carbon steel, aluminum alloy, and copper) in a variety of corrosive conditions (such as acidic solutions, and saltwater). In addition, as compared to conventional organic corrosion inhibitors, N, S-CDs exhibit low toxicity and environmental compatibility, hence avoiding significant environmental contamination. Most prominently, N, S-CDs are doped by the introduction of N and S, giving them physicochemical properties that are different from those of traditional CDs. It not only changes the electronic structure of CDs but also enhances their surface activity, giving them unique advantages in the field of metal corrosion.

In this work, we proposed a new environmentally friendly, green, and efficient N, S-CDs corrosion inhibitor, synthesized starting from o-phenylenediamine and amidinothiourea as precursors, characterized by XPS, FTIR, Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy, and thermogravimetric analysis/differential thermal analysis (TGA/DTA). The anti-corrosive behavior of N, S-CDs at different concentrations on the Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution was evaluated using EIS and PDP tests. In addition, scanning electron microscopy (SEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), and 3D profilometer were used to analyze the surface morphology of carbon steel at different levels of corrosion, the change of corrosion current on the surface of carbon steel was also analyzed by SECM, and the hydrophilic behavior of the steel surface was analyzed by contact angle measurement (CAM). Finally, XPS and ATR-FTIR were used to analyze the inhibitor-modified steel surface, and the interaction between doped elements and the metal substrate was further explored. At the same time, combined with the fitting of adsorption isotherms, the inhibition mechanism of N, S-CDs was revealed.

The experiments were carried out with o-phenylenediamine and amidinothiourea as precursors, absolute ethanol, and hydrochloric acid as experimental reagents. The experimental consumables and chemical reagents were purchased from the corresponding manufacturers. O-phenylenediamine and amidinothiourea were acquired from Aladdin (Shanghai, China), absolute alcohol, concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37 %, AR) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China), all chemical reagents can be used directly without special treatment, and ultrapure water was used during the whole test. The working electrode adopted Q235 carbon steel (1 cm × 1 cm × 1 cm). Before the experiment, the working electrodes were polished sequentially with dry and wet sandpaper of 120, 240, 400, 600, 800, 1000, and 2000 mesh, respectively. Then, the surface contaminants were eliminated with deionized water in the ultrasonic cleaning machine, and the surface lipid substances were removed with ethanol solution for 1 min, and then dried with cold air for the experiment.

As illustrated in Fig. 1, N, S-CDs were prepared via hydrothermal method. O-phenylenediamine (1 g) and amidinothiourea (1 g) were evenly blended in 80 mL of absolute alcohol. The mixture was then injected into the reaction kettle, heated at 180 ℃ for 16 h, and subsequently cooled to room temperature. Subsequently, a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 1000 Da for 48 h was used to get rid of any unreacted substance. Eventually, the residual solvent was removed by rotary evaporation, and the dialysate was freeze-dried for 72 h to give a brown solid.

The molecular structure of the N, S-CDs was tested by FTIR (IRAffinity-1) and UV–vis (TU-1901) spectroscopy. DTA-TGA analyses were carried out with apparatus type Shimadzu DTG-60 H by increasing temperature from room temperature to 800 °C with 10 °C/min intervals. X-ray diffraction spectrometer (XRD, DX-2700) was used to analyze the crystalline state of N, S-CDs, and TEM (JEM-2010) was used to observe its size distribution and morphology. XPS (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha) was performed to analyze the coordination mode and the interaction sites of the carbon dots on the metal surface.

Electrochemical tests were executed using the CHI660E electrochemical workstation with a traditional three-electrode system comprising a saturated calomel electrode, platinum sheet (auxiliary electrode), and Q235 carbon steel (working electrode). To assess the system’s stability, a preliminary open circuit potential (OCP) experiment was conducted, after the potential was stabilized, the next step of the experiment was carried out. Following, During the EIS experiment, the applied voltage was the stable OCP value, the frequency was set to 10−2 Hz-105 Hz, and the interference signal was covered by 5 mV. EIS results were then analyzed using the ZSimpWin software. PDP curves were executed within the range from −250 mV to 250 mV relative to OCP with a scanning rate of 0.5 mV s−1. All tests were conducted repeat three times to increase data credibility and reduce experimental error, ensuring consistency over all test conditions.

The variation of surface corrosion current intensity of Q235 steel was analyzed by SECM (CHI920D) (the diagram is shown in Fig. S1). With and without the inhibitor, the surface morphology of Q235 steel in 1 M HCl was analyzed using SEM (JSM-7800 F). To evaluate the corrosion inhibition mechanism of N, S-CDs, the elemental ingredient, and the surface bonding state of the sample were assessed using XPS analysis. The roughness and hydrophobicity of the Q235 steel surface were determined by AFM (MFP-3D-BIO), 3D profilometer (AM-7000), and CAM (SCI2000B). The molecular structure of the N, S-CDs was analyzed using ATR-FTIR (IRTracer-100+AIM-9000). Additionally, a host ATR accessory was employed for testing and comparing the surface-modified steel sheet.

Combined with density functional theory (DFT) and MD simulations, the electronic structure and adsorption mode of N, S-CDs were researched [17,18]. Calculations were performed with the GGA-BLYP and B3LYP exchange-correlation functionals and the dual-numeric quality and polarization (DNP) basis set by using Material Studio (MS) software [19], and the effect of water in the solvent was solved by employing an implicit conductor-like shielding model (COSMO, dielectric constant 78.54) [20]. During the calculation, the convergence thresholds for energy, maximum force, and displacement were set at 1 × 10−5 Ha, 2 × 10−3 Ha Å−1, and 5 × 10−3 Å, respectively. The adsorption model of N, S-CDs corrosion inhibitors on carbon steel surface was built by the Forcite module in the MS software package. The calculations process was carried out in a simulation box (27.3×27.3×33.1 Å3) with periodic boundary conditions, consisting of a Fe (110) basis and a solvent layer (800 H2O + one N, S-CDs monomer). Previous studies have proved that the Fe (110) surface has an extremely stable and stacked structure, so we selected the Fe (110) surface for simulation calculations [21]. The Fe (110) surface was represented by a five-layer plate model, with each layer standing as one (11 × 11) unit cell. The simulation process was executed under an Andersen thermostat and NVT ensemble, the simulation time was 8000 ps, the time step was 1.0 fs, and the temperature was controlled by an Andersen thermostat at 298 K.

FTIR spectroscopy is employed to examine o-phenylenediamine, amidinothiourea, and the prepared N, S-CDs to determine whether N and S were elements successfully doped. In Fig. 2a, the green line represents the spectrum of amidinothiourea, the peaks at 3436 cm−1 and 3303 cm−1 are associated with vibrational stretching, as well as the peak at 1621 cm−1 is attributed to the shear motion of −NH2 functional group. Besides, the peak at approximately 1493 cm−1 can be associated with the vibrational stretching of the CS group [22]. In the FTIR spectrum of o-phenylenediamine, peaks are observed at 3385, 1630, and 1589 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibrations of −NH2, CC, and benzene rings, respectively [23,24], while the peak at 749 cm−1 is associated with out-of-plane bending vibration of benzenic C-H [25]. For pure N, S-CDs, the broadband and peak around 3111 cm−1 is associated with the O-H vibration [26], indicating the presence of hydroxyl groups in the structure of N, S-CDs. Additionally, the peak at about 2978 cm−1 can be ascribed to the asymmetric stretching vibration of the C-H group [27]. At about 2061 cm−1, the characteristic absorptive peak of the double bonds NCS and NCO can be observed [28]. The peaks at approximately 1734 cm−1, 1365 cm−1, 1200 cm−1, and 1028 cm−1 correspond to the tensile vibration of CO, C-N, both C-S/CS [29,30], C-O bonds [31], respectively. Simply, all spectra confirm the successful doping of N and S atoms.

The thermogravimetric curves of N, S-CDs powder in an air environment are shown in Fig. 2b. TGA measures the relationship between the mass of a sample and temperature and time under a controlled temperature program and atmosphere to obtain the variation of the sample's mass with temperature. The TGA curve reveals two distinct weight-loss processes, in which the weight loss rate is 72.43 % in the temperature range from 33°C to 400°C, which is attributed to the removal of chemisorption water [32]. The weight loss rate is 25.95 % over the temperature range from 400°C to 800°C, and the total weight loss rate is 98.38 % over the entire temperature range. Four endothermic peaks are observed in the DTA curve, corresponding to the overall mass loss. Among them, a small endothermic peak at 285°C occurs in the first stage, which is attributed to the removal of chemisorption water on N, S-CDs, which is consistent with the discussion of TGA. Three endothermic peaks at 475°C, 556°C, and 620°C appear in the second stage, attributed to the formation of N, S-CDs due to the presence of a small amount of raw material [33]. In the XRD spectra of N, S-CDs in Fig. 2c, diffraction peaks exist at 2θ = 23.13°, indicating that N, S-CDs have been successfully prepared and have high crystallinity. The TEM image in Fig. 2d shows the morphology and size of N, S-CDs, and the presence of spherical nanoparticles with inhomogeneous graphene-like can be clearly observed, and the particle size distribution of N, S-CDs is shown in Fig. S2 with an average quantum size of 2.65 nm.

XPS detection method is executed to analyze the composition and valence of the synthesized N, S-CDs. The spectrum reported in Fig. 3a exhibits peaks at 284.23, 531.28, 399.20, and 162.73 eV, corresponding to C 1 s (68.37 at%), N 1 s (17.64 at%), O 1 s (11.71 at%), and S 2p (2.28 at%), respectively. The C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3b) shows the presence of CC (284.8 eV), C-N, C-O, and C-S (285.65 eV), as well as CO/CS/CN (288.76 eV) covalent bonds. In the N 1 s spectrum (see Fig. 3c) two peaks are observed at 398.75 and 400.34 eV, corresponding to pyridinic N and pyrrolic N atoms [28]. The deconvolution of O 1 s displays two peaks centered at 532.1 and 533.1 eV, attributed to C-O-C and adsorbed oxygen species, respectively (see Fig. 3d) [34]. Regarding the S 2p spectrum, two peaks at 162.33 and 168.64 eV are assigned to C-S and C-SO x bonds, respectively (see Fig. 3e) [35]. These results confirm that the N, S-CDs display well water solubility and have been successfully doped with N and S atoms.

In Fig. 3f, the UV-Vis absorption spectrum reveals a distinct peak at 282 nm, which may be attributed to the n-π* transition doped with heteroatoms [36]. Consequently, it is presumed that the structure of N, S-CDs contains heteroatomic functional groups. Thus, when irradiated at a wavelength of 365 nm in the ultraviolet area, the aqueous solution containing the molecule exhibits purple fluorescence, showing that N, S-CDs have excellent fluorescence.

EIS is commonly used to investigate electrochemical reactions at the metal and solution interface. Fig. 4a and b illustrate the Nyquist and Bode curves of Q235 carbon steel immersed in 1 M HCl solution, after adding different concentrations of N, S-CDs. In Fig. 4a, the Nyquist diagram relative to the blank signal displays a concave semicircular arc, primarily attributed to the unevenness of the carbon steel surface. Upon successive additions of 100 mg/L of N, S-CDs corrosion inhibitor, the diameter of the semicircular signal obviously increases, resulting in a reduction of the metal corrosion. As shown in Fig. 4b, with the gradual increase of N and S-CDs concentration, the impedance modulus values in the phase angle diagram and bode diagram increase significantly, indicating that the addition of N, S-CDs effectively alleviates the corrosion of carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution.

The results of the equivalent circuit fitting are reported in Table 1. Here, the χ 2 value is in the range of 0.461×10−3∼1.750×10−3, which indicates that the equivalent circuit fits well with the actual reaction. The R ct value progressively increases with the increase of N, S-CDs concentration, inhibiting electron transfer and reducing metal corrosion [45]. In addition, with the concentration of N, S-CDs increases, the capacitance C f and C dl of the electric double layer decrease. Hence, as also suggested by the Helmholtz mode, this phenomenon occurs due to the adsorption of N, S-CDs on the Q235 steel surface, increasing the thickness of the electric double layer and isolating the reaction between electrode and solution, thereby inhibiting the corrosion of the metal. The corrosion inhibition efficiency of carbon steel by adding trace amounts of N, S-CDs is as high as 92.4 %, indicating that the corrosion of Q235 carbon steel is effectively inhibited in 1 M HCl. Table S1 provides a comparison of the efficacy of our CDs with other sources in the literature. Our product has higher effectiveness at lower doses. Furthermore, the EIS results shown in Fig. S3 and Table S2 demonstrate the stability of the inhibitor layer in corrosive medium, as the inhibition efficacy remains unchanged even after varying immersion periods at the optimum concentration.

PDP curves of Q235 steel in the presence and absence of N, S-CDs are presented in Fig. 5. All electrochemical parameters and inhibition efficiency are collected in Table 2. E corr and i corr express the corrosion potential and corrosion current density, respectively; β a and β b are the relative slopes of the anodic and cathodic polarization curves, tangent to the intersection, respectively; θ expresses the metal surface coverage, while η Tafel expresses the corrosion inhibition efficiency. These data can be derived from the following equations [46]:

and i corr represent the corrosion current densities in the absence and presence of corrosion inhibitors, respectively. According to the data reported in Table 2, as the inhibitor concentration increases, the i corr decreases significantly. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 5, as the concentration of N, S-CDs increases, the slope of the cathodic polarization curve tends to be parallel to that of the blank solution, showing that the corrosion inhibitor does not impact the cathodic reaction. However, the presence of N, S-CDs increases the reduction overpotential of hydrogen ions, thereby inhibiting the ion migration at the interface and retarding the corrosion rate. On the other hand, in the presence of the molecules, the anodic polarization curve fluctuates greatly, and the corresponding β a value shows an overall downward trend. This indicates that the inhibitory effect mainly involves the anodic reaction at the metal interface, where the adsorbed inhibitor hinders electron migration, thereby improving the corrosion inhibition efficiency.

The electrochemical test results show that N, S-CDs can effectively enhance the corrosion resistance of Q235 carbon steel in an acidic environment. To verify the corrosion inhibition effect of N, S-CDs in an acidic environment, it is essential to explore the corrosion protection mechanism. The corrosion potential E corr is given by the Nernst equation as [47]:

represents standard corrosion potential. Combined with the Nernst equation, it can be deduced that the E corr should decrease due to the continuous consumption of H+ and the formation of Fe2+, so the E corr moves to the negative side, reducing corrosion current, thereby reducing corrosion rate and improving corrosion inhibition efficiency, that is consistent with the results of PDP curve.

Fig. 6 shows the SECM test plot of Q235 carbon steel after 6 h immersion in 1 M HCl with and without corrosion inhibitors at room temperature. As shown in Fig. 6a, the current value increases to 0.696 nA when the probe is close to the carbon steel surface without inhibitors, which reflects the higher conductivity of the surface of the steel and solution. Since the anodic corrosion product of iron is Fe2+, the metal dissolving activity is enhanced in the pitting and intergranular corrosion regions, so the Fe2+ concentration increases, and the current detected by the probe is higher. In addition, the effects of Cl− cause pitting and intergranular corrosion to continue to develop longitudinally over time, leading to more severe pitting and intergranular corrosion [48]. The test plot of the sample containing the corrosion inhibitor shows a small change in current compared to the sample without the corrosion inhibitor. In Fig. 6b, when the probe is close to the steel surface containing N, S-CDs, the current value increases to 0.087 nA, and the current value decreases by almost an order of magnitude after adding N, S-CDs, suggesting that the steel surface may form a dense protective film through N, S-CDs, which inhibits heterogeneous electron migrate and impedes further anodic dissolution of Fe. The test results indicate that N, S-CDs had excellent corrosion inhibition effects.

SEM and 3D Profilometer microscopic images show the Q235 carbon steel surface alone ( Fig. 7a, d), after soaking in a blank solution (Fig. 7b, e), and after soaking in the solution with the added N, S-CDs for 6 h (Fig. 7c, f). Before wetting the polished Q235 carbon steel, its surface appears relatively smooth. After immersion in the blank solution, severe signs of corrosion form on the surface. Finally, pitting is inhibited by solution containing the N, S-CDs, making the surface relatively flat, and confirming the corrosion inhibition effect of the molecule.

The microscopic topography and wettability of the carbon steel surface are observed by AFM and CAM, respectively, and the results are reported in Fig. 8a∼c. Here, the 2D and 3D surface topography of the Q235 carbon steel substrate is shown under the three conditions: basis, in the absence (blank solution), and the presence of N, S-CDs. As depicted in the figure, the polished carbon steel exhibits a flat surface with a relative roughness of 25.1 nm (Fig. 8a). The 3D and 2D (line scan) topographies of the surface soaking in a blank solution reveal multiple bulges, whose depth fluctuates greatly, increasing the roughness up to 136.1 nm (Fig. 8b). With the addition of corrosion inhibitors, the metal surface significantly flattens, showing a more uniform depth distribution and a substantial reduction in surface roughness down to 41.5 nm (Fig. 8c). Thus, the corrosion inhibitor displays its effective role by forming a uniform protective film on the carbon steel surface. Fig. 8d shows the contact angles of 66°, 29°, and 42° for the polished, unsuppressed, and suppressed samples, respectively. Corroded samples in a blank solution exhibit a lower contact angle, which is due to the easier diffusion of water droplets due to corrosion. However, upon the addition of the corrosion inhibitor, the contact angle of the sample increased to 42°, indicating that the corrosion inhibitor formed a protection film on the steel, increasing the surface hydrophobicity.

The adsorption dynamics of N, S-CDs on Q235 steel are analyzed by the XPS method. As shown in Fig. 9a, the analysis of surface composition indicates the presence of C (46.69 at%), N (2.22 at%), O (41.14 at%), Fe (8.47 at%), and S (1.48 at%) on the steel surface, due to the interactive with the inhibitor that adsorbs on the surface by forming a protection film. High-resolution spectra of the C 1 s, N 1 s, O1s, Fe 2p, and S 2p peaks are reported in the Fig. 9(b-f). The C 1 s spectrum shows three peaks at 284.8, 286.3, and 288.6 eV corresponding to C-C, C-N/C-O/C-S, and covalent bond of CO/CS/CN, respectively [49]. The high-resolution N 1 s spectrum displays two peaks, at 398.7 and 399.8 eV, corresponding to C-N and =N-, respectively [50]. The O 1 s spectrum shows two peaks at 529.8 and 532.7 eV which are counterparts to Fe2O3 and C-O, respectively [51]. The spectrum of Fe 2p displays five peaks at 706.6, 709.9, 711.4, 723.5, and 726.1 eV. Fe 2p3/2 is related to metallic iron Fe, S-Fe, and Fe2O3/Fe3O4, respectively, while Fe 2p1/2 proves the presence of FeOOH iron complexes [52,53]. The S 2p spectrum shows four peaks at 162.1, 163.2, 168.03, and 169.1 eV, corresponding to S-Fe (S 2p3/2), C-S (S 2p3/2), CS (S 2p1/2), and C-SO x (S 2p1/2) [54], respectively. The XPS test results proved the presence of the chemically adsorbed N, S-CDs on the Q235 carbon steel surface, which forms a protection film with different iron oxide products to inhibit corrosion.

The surface-modified steel sheets are compared and analyzed by ATR-FTIR to verify whether the N, S-CDs powder standard was assembled onto the steel sheet. The spectra of pure N, S-CDs, bare steel, and steel sheets with N, S-CDs-doped surface are shown in Fig. 10. For pure N, S-CDs, at about 2061.90 cm−1, the depicted absorption peak of the cumulative double bond is divided into NCS and NCO. The tensile vibration of C-N can be observed at 1365.60 cm−1, while the peak at about 1200 cm−1 corresponds to C-S and CS bonds. The bare steel, on the contrary, does not exhibit significant absorption peaks. In summary, the surface modification of the steel sheet with N, S-CDs shows an infrared absorption signal that overlaps with pure N, S-CDs. Anyway, the spectral intensity is a little different from that of pure N, S-CDs, resulting in a slight wavenumber shift.

The adsorption mechanism of N, S-CDs on Q235 steel, acting as corrosion inhibitors, can be described through isotherms. These parameters are models representing the amount of adsorbates accumulated on the surface, as a function of their concentration. Three types of adsorption isotherms can be used to explore the interaction between Q235 steel and N, S-CDs: Langmuir, Frumkin, and Temkin. The formulas for adsorption isotherms by following [55–57].

where C inh is the corrosion inhibitor concentration, K ads is the adsorption isotherm constant, and θ is the surface coverage. EIS figures are used to fit the three isotherms and, as shown in Fig. 11(a-c), the Langmuir model, having a fitted R 2 value of 0.99995, displays a better agreement concerning the Frumkin (R 2 = 0.96587) and Temkin (R 2 = 0.97252) adsorption isotherms. After comparison, the adsorption process of the corrosion inhibitor conforms to the Langmuir adsorption model.

Quantum chemical calculations are used to further explore the donor-acceptor interactions between N, S-CDs and Q235 carbon steel. As shown in Fig. 12a, the electronic distribution of N, S-CDs in HOMO and LUMO is described. It can be clearly seen that the HOMO and LUMO areas are primarily distributed on the aromatic six-membered rings, and the electrons in the HOME region are more densely distributed, indicating that there are more electrons in the HOMO region, which are shared with the steel surface to form chemical bonds between the corrosion inhibitor and the steel surface. On the other hand, the adsorption isotherm results show that N, S-CDs can be adsorbed on metal surfaces through physical and chemical adsorption mechanisms, and the HOMO region is the physical and chemical adsorption center from the results of molecular orbital analysis of the N, S-CDs front. In contrast, the LUMO region has less electron distribution because the LUMO region is responsible for the electrical acceptor properties of the corrosion inhibitor, and some d-electrons are retransferred from the iron d-orbital to the LUMO region, which also props up the adsorption of N, S-CDs on the steel surface.( Fig. 13)

The adsorption of N, S-CDs on Fe (110) surface was theoretically studied by MD simulation. As shown in Fig. 12b, the most favorable adsorption centers (top and side views) of N, S-CDs on the Fe (110) surface are described. Fig. 12c indicates the density field distribution of N, S-CDs on Fe (110) basal, indicating that a dense protective layer is formed in the three-dimensional space, which effectively resists the erosion of corrosive substances. The relative concentration distribution of H2O molecules during the MD simulation is shown in Fig. 12d, we can clearly observe the peak of the highest relative concentration of H2O molecules containing N, S-CDs is obviously lower than the blank condition, indicating the inhibitor is more inclined to adsorb on metal surfaces.

The intensity of the corrosion inhibitor can be assessed by adsorption energy (E ads), the value can be acquired by the following equation [60]:
(17)
E
ads
=
E
total
−
(
E
surf + solu
+
E
inh + solu
)
+
E
solu
where E total represents the whole system energy, E solu represents the potential energy of the solvent molecule, E surf+solu represents the total energy of the Fe (110) surface and the solution without corrosion inhibitor, and E inh+solu represents the total energy of the solution with corrosion inhibitor. In general, the more negative the E ads value, the stronger the adsorption strength between the corrosion inhibitor molecule and the metal surface. The E ads value of N, S-CDs corrosion inhibitor is calculated as −435.6 kJ/mol, which confirms the stability of the adsorption system and can be expected to spontaneous adsorption. In summary, our theoretical results further verify the tested results.

Based on the tested results, a supposed model can be put forward to explain the inhibition mechanism of N, S-CDs against steel corrosion. In general, the adsorption of inhibitors on steel surfaces occurs via the replacement of already present water molecules. In hydrochloric acid solution (blank), the surface acquires a positive charge. When the inhibiter is added, nitrogen atoms become protonated by the acidic environment. Adsorption is further enhanced due to the presence of Cl- ions bound to metal atoms making the surface more negative. This is because Cl− establishes electrostatic interactions with the charged N, S-CD, acting as a bridge between the protonated inhibitor and the negatively charged steel surface [61]. Regarding chemical adsorption, it is found from the XPS test results that the doped N and S elements are good electron donors to form coordination bonds with iron atoms, improving the adsorption of the inhibitor on the steel surface.

In this work, a new environmentally friendly N, S-CDs corrosion inhibitor is successfully prepared by hydrothermal method using o-phenylenediamine and amidinothiourea as precursors. Combined with systematic experimental tests, data analysis, and computational simulations, the corrosion inhibition properties of N, S-CDs on Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution are studied. We can find that due to the addition of N, S-CDs, the corrosion of steel in hydrochloric acid is significantly weakened, and a high corrosion inhibition efficiency is achieved, which provides a strong basis and scheme for the design of additives in the metal pickling industry, and the conclusions are summarized as follows:

(1) The structure of N, S-CDs is characterized by FTIR, XRD, TEM, and XPS, and the results show that N, S-CDs is a graphene-like structure with a size of about 2.65 nm and contains abundant N, S groups.

(2) EIS test data shows that N, S-CDs can act as an effective corrosion inhibitor to protect Q235 steel from corrosion in a 1 M HCl solution. By increasing the concentration of N, S-CDs, the impedance arc increases significantly, the corrosion inhibitor shows excellent activity at 100 mg/L, and the efficiency reaches 92.4 %. The Tafel results show that N, S-CDs is an excellent mixed corrosion inhibitor, and its corrosion inhibition efficiency is consistent with the EIS results.

(3) The SECM results show that the current value is reduced by nearly an order of magnitude after adding N, S-CDs, indicating that a dense protection film can be formed on the metal surface by N, S-CDs, which can inhibit heterogeneous electron migrate and prevent further anodic dissolution of Fe, and effectively inhibit the corrosion of Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl. The surface analysis shows that the formation of the adsorption film on the steel surface by N, S-CDs effectively reduces the interfacial corrosion.
(4) As confirmed by XPS and ATR-FTIR results, N, S-CDs adsorb on the steel surface, through the formation of coordination bonds between the heteroatoms of the inhibiter and the metal atoms.

(5) The calculation of adsorption isotherms confirms that N, S-CDs adsorb onto the surface of Q235 carbon steel through physical adsorption and chemical adsorption mechanisms, in line with the Langmuir adsorption model.
(6) The theoretical calculations results show that N, S-CDs molecules spontaneously adsorb on the Fe (110) surface.
